findings.title: Zero-sum scope categories erase nonzero leaf subtrees
findings.claim: A category whose aggregate is zero is omitted even when it contains nonzero positive and negative function rows. This violates the documented rule that a row may be omitted only when both its aggregate and its entire rendered subtree are zero.
findings.reproduction: Using .goalloop-support/triage6/Q3/profile.pb, I ran both CLIs with cost_kind Work matching name:Pos and name:Neg, and scope S rolling Work into All. Both exited 0 and emitted boundary S with total_time_ns 0, samples 2, and "buckets": []. A direct Python analysis probe printed {'aggregate': 0, 'leaf_functions': {'Pos': 10, 'Neg': -10}} followed by []. Thus the +10 and -10 leaf rows exist but are erased by the category-level zero check.

findings.title: Scope slice predicates ignore effective first-match ownership
findings.claim: A scope predicate such as slice:B tests whether B's path rule matches a frame, rather than whether B actually owns that frame after first-match slice resolution. Overlapping definitions therefore attribute scope data to slices that never own it.
findings.reproduction: I supplied slices A and B with identical /srv/app/** paths. `python -m clankerprof.cli slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json --slices /dev/fd/9` exited 0 and assigned all 7 ns to A, confirming first-match ownership. I then ran Python and Rust `scopes` with the same slice file, owner `BOwner: slice:B`, and scope function T. Both exited 0 and emitted domain BOwner with time_ns 7 and pct 100.0, although B owns no frame.

findings.title: Python silently accepts a misspelled top-level slices key
findings.claim: The Python slice loader defaults a missing `slices` member to an empty list, so a typo silently disables the supplied configuration. Rust correctly rejects the same input, breaking validation parity.
findings.reproduction:
I ran `.venv/bin/python -m clankerprof.cli slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json --slices <(printf 'slice:
  - name: app
    paths: [/srv/app/**]
')`. Python exited 0 and emitted a normal report assigning 7 ns to implicit slice `(all)`. The equivalent Rust command exited 2 with `{"error": "Slices file must contain a slices array.", "ok": false}`.

findings.title: A present facts-v2 summary can be empty and evade cross-validation
findings.claim: Both importers treat missing members inside a present summary object as absent checks. Consequently `summary: {}` is accepted exactly like no summary, despite the contract saying a present summary is redundant data to be cross-validated.
findings.reproduction: I replaced the summary in .goalloop-support/triage4/K14/facts_valid.json with `{}` and passed the result to Python and Rust `slices --facts`. Both exited 0 and emitted the ordinary 7 ns slice report. No summary field was required or checked, so a present but wholly truncated summary passes validation.

findings.title: Mixed global and local --output options use the first value instead of the last
findings.claim: The global-option hoisting logic moves a leading global `--output` after the subcommand's later local value. Since scalar options are last-wins, this reverses the user's ordering and selects the earlier path.
findings.reproduction: I ran both CLIs as `--output .goalloop-support compare --before .goalloop-support/triage4/K13/slice_report.json --after .goalloop-support/triage4/K13/slice_report.json --output .goalloop-support/round8-local/out.json`. Both selected the earlier directory rather than the later file. Python exited 2 with `{"error": "[Errno 21] Is a directory: '.goalloop-support'", "ok": false}`; Rust exited 2 with `{"error": "Is a directory (os error 21)", "ok": false}`.

findings.title: Both protobuf decoders reject legal unknown group fields
findings.claim: The protobuf skip routines support wire types 0, 1, 2, and 5 but reject the legal deprecated group wire types 3 and 4. A valid pprof containing an unknown group is therefore classified as malformed.
findings.reproduction: I prefixed the valid profile .goalloop-support/triage/F4/base.pb with bytes `\x7b\x7c`, an empty unknown group for field 15, and ran `facts --profile` in both CLIs. Both exited 2 with `{"error": "Unsupported protobuf wire type: 3", "ok": false}` instead of skipping the unknown field and decoding the profile.

findings.title: Scope loading bypasses duplicate and reserved slice validation
findings.claim: Slice validation is performed by slice analysis but not when a slice file is loaded through scope configuration. Invalid duplicate names, multiple defaults, and reserved pseudo-slice names can therefore enter the scope matcher.
findings.reproduction: The existing .goalloop-support/triage5/P7/slices.yml contains two slices named App. I ran both `scopes --facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json` implementations with a config referencing that file and defining scope function T. Both exited 0 and emitted a normal T report totaling 7 ns. The documented duplicate-name error was never raised on this loading path.

findings.title: Rust's numeric-backreference rewrite miscounts parentheses inside regex comments
findings.claim: The Rust compatibility rewrite counts a parenthesis inside `(?#...)` as a capture group. It consequently fails to rewrite a valid Python numeric backreference and rejects a pattern outside the documented engine-semantic exceptions.
findings.reproduction: Python `re.compile(r'(?P<a>/srv)(?#()(?P<b>/app)\2')` reported 2 groups and matched `/srv/app/app`. I changed the fixture path to `/srv/app/app` and configured target Matched with that regex. Python exited 0 and assigned 7 ns/100% to Matched. Rust exited 2 with `Invalid regex pattern ... Numbered backref/call not allowed because named group was used, use a named backref instead`.

findings.title: Bottom slice-filter rescue ignores attribute-rule precedence
findings.claim: When evaluating a bottom `slice:<name>` filter, both implementations scan for any descendant attribute rule targeting that slice rather than resolving the first matching attribute rule. A later, losing rule can therefore make the filter pass.
findings.reproduction: I ran both `slices` CLIs on .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json with empty slices A and B, attributes `<name:Leaf,to:A` followed by `<name:T,to:B`, and `--filter slice:B`. Both exited 0 and emitted 7 ns under A with matching_time_ns 7. The first descendant rule makes A the effective attribution, so filtering for B should have rejected the sample.

findings.title: Python silently treats an explicit empty --output as no option
findings.claim: Multiple Python command paths guard `args.output` by truthiness. An explicitly supplied empty path is therefore ignored and the full artifact is written to stdout, while Rust rejects the value as a usage error.
findings.reproduction: `.venv/bin/python -m clankerprof.cli slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json --output ''` exited 0 and printed the complete report, including the 7 ns `(all)` slice, to stdout. The equivalent Rust command exited 2 with `{"error": "error: a value is required for '--output <OUTPUT>' but none was supplied\n\nFor more information, try '--help'.", "ok": false}`.

findings.title: The --show-paths flag has no observable effect
findings.claim: Both renderers always include frame filenames and never consult the stored `show_paths` option. The parity test only compares two equally inert implementations, so it does not verify the advertised behavior.
findings.reproduction: I compared Python slice output for .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json with and without `--show-paths`; `cmp -s` returned 0, and the no-flag output already contained `"filename": "/srv/app/leaf.py"`. Repeating the comparison with Rust also returned 0. Thus toggling the flag changes no bytes.

findings.title: Unknown top-level scope keys silently disable configuration sections
findings.claim: Unknown-key validation covers individual scope entries but not the scope configuration's top level. A typo in `owner`, `cost_kind`, or another section is ignored and can silently remove attribution or exclusions.
findings.reproduction: I ran both scope CLIs with facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json and config `owenr: {Intended: name:Leaf}; scope: [{function: T}]`. Both exited 0 and emitted a normal report with `"domains": []`; the misspelled owner section was silently discarded instead of producing a validation error.

findings.title: Repeated protobuf period_type messages are replaced instead of merged
findings.claim: Protobuf singular embedded-message fields merge repeated occurrences, but both decoders overwrite the previous `period_type`. Valid split-field encodings therefore lose metadata.
findings.reproduction: I passed both facts exporters the handcrafted valid profile bytes `\x32\x00\x32\x03cpu\x32\x0bnanoseconds\x5a\x02\x08\x01\x5a\x02\x10\x02`. The two period_type occurrences respectively contain type index 1 and unit index 2. Both CLIs exited 0 and emitted `"period_type":{"type":"","unit":"nanoseconds"}`; protobuf merge semantics require `{"type":"cpu","unit":"nanoseconds"}`.

findings.title: Facts-v2 accepts a primary index inconsistent with the declared default metric
findings.claim: The R7 bounds check ensures only that `primary_value_index` is in range; neither importer verifies that it equals the deterministic index selected from `default_sample_type` and `value_types`. Self-consistent but semantically contradictory facts select the wrong metric.
findings.reproduction: Starting from .goalloop-support/triage4/K14/facts_valid.json, I set value_types to count/count then cpu/nanoseconds, default_sample_type to `count`, primary_value_index to 1, sample values to [1,100], and summary total_primary_value to 100. Both `slices --facts` implementations exited 0 and emitted time_ns 100. The selection contract names count at index 0, so this contradictory artifact should be rejected rather than analyzed using index 1.

summary: Reproduced 14 new findings: 11 major and 3 minor. No previously refuted finding was re-raised. The charter gates could not be independently completed under the mandated read-only sandbox: `./bin/dev check` failed when `mktemp` could not create its temporary file, and `cargo test --workspace` failed opening `target/debug/.cargo-build-lock` with permission denied. Those environmental failures are audit limitations, not repository findings.
